intent: Key collision (catalog #31): two different rules (execve HIGH, init_module/finit_module LOW) sharing one -k access. Per-key aggregation must attribute correctly and not conflate the two rules' volumes as a single rule.

rules.note: unrestricted execve HIGH. Shares key=access with the module rule but is a DISTINCT rule.
module-load syscalls, rare => LOW. SAME key=access. Must NOT be conflated with the execve rule despite the shared key.

suspected_trap: Two rules share -k access but are distinct (execve HIGH vs module-load LOW). A model that assumes a key uniquely identifies a rule will conflate them: it may count only one rule's volume under the key, or mis-attribute the combined per-key total to a single rule's tier. Correct behavior: per-RULE tiering keeps execve=HIGH and modules=LOW distinct, while --from-log per-KEY aggregation may legitimately SUM both into the access bucket (f3 section 7 item 1). The rule-vs-key distinction is the load-bearing logic.
